text:
🦔April CPI came in at 0.6% month-over-month and 3.8% year-over-year, the highest annual reading since May 2023 and 0.1 percentage point above consensus. Core CPI excluding food and energy rose 0.4% on the month and 2.8% annually. Energy prices jumped 3.8% on the month with gasoline up 28.4% year over year as the Iran conflict continues to disrupt oil supply. Shelter rose 0.6%, apparel rose 0.6%, airline fares accelerated 2.8%, and household furnishings rose 0.7%, indicating tariff pressure across multiple categories. 
Real average hourly wages fell 0.5% on the month and 0.3% annually, meaning wage gains are being fully eaten by inflation for the first time in three years. Fed rate hike odds by year-end jumped to 30% on the print.

My Take
Two separate inflation engines are running at the same time, which is what makes this print harder for the Fed than the 2022 episode was. Energy is the obvious driver with oil above $100 a barrel and gasoline at $4.50 nationally, but tariff pass-through is showing up in apparel, furnishings, and airline fares simultaneously, which means the price pressure is broader than a single supply shock. The Fed can wait out an energy spike. It cannot wait out a structural increase in goods costs while wages are losing ground in real terms.

The setup heading into the next FOMC meeting is genuinely difficult. Kevin Warsh has signaled he wants lower rates, four dissents at the April meeting was the highest count since 1992, and the labor market data is soft enough to argue for cuts. The CPI print pulls in the opposite direction hard enough that the market is now pricing in a 30% chance of hikes by year-end, which is not where anyone expected to be six months ago. If you are managing personal finances around this, the takeaways are straightforward. Variable-rate debt is going to stay expensive. Mortgage rates are not coming down quickly. Cash holdings in high-yield savings continue to earn meaningful real returns. And if you are invested in long-duration assets like growth stocks or long bonds, the inflation print is the kind of data that historically resets those positions lower before the Fed catches up.

text:
🦔NHS England is granting Palantir staff and other external contractors "admin" roles with unlimited access to identifiable patient data on the federated data platform, according to an internal briefing reported by the Financial Times. The change replaces a case-by-case approval process, with the briefing noting external workers had requested broader permissions because "it is too inconvenient to apply for all of the necessary individual CDAs." 
Palantir signed a £330 million contract in 2023 to build the platform, which connects data across NHS organisations covering roughly 67 million people. Cybersecurity experts warn the change leaves the system "one admin compromise away from a data breach of unseen proportions."

My Take
The phrase that should jump out at every reader is "too inconvenient to apply for all of the necessary individual CDAs." The case-by-case approval process existed for a reason, which is that identifiable health records are among the most sensitive data any government holds about its citizens. The convenience of contractors is now being prioritized over the security of 67 million people's medical histories, and the briefing document admits this in writing.

Public health data is one of the most commercially valuable datasets on the planet, and once a private contractor has admin-level visibility into a national health system, the institutional knowledge they walk away with is worth more than the £330 million contract itself. Palantir says it can only process data on the customer's instruction, which is technically accurate, but the company also builds the access logs, the analytics tooling, and the operational map of how the data moves. That is the kind of position you do not let go of voluntarily once you have it.

text:
🦔Google's developers significantly understated the carbon emissions of two proposed UK data centers in their planning documents, according to analysis by the tech justice nonprofit Foxglove. The Thurrock and North Weald facilities in Essex compared one year of emissions against the UK's entire five-year carbon budget, making the impact appear five times smaller than reality. Greystoke's planned data center in north Lincolnshire contains the same error. 
The Thurrock facility alone will emit roughly 570,000 tonnes of CO2 annually, more than an international airport. Foxglove's broader analysis found that just ten of the largest UK data centers in planning will produce 2.7 million tonnes of annual emissions, which would wipe out the entire 2.9 million tonne carbon savings expected from the UK's switch to electric cars in 2025.

My Take
This is not a rounding error. Comparing one year of emissions to five years of the national carbon budget is exactly the calculation you would run if you wanted the number to look five times smaller, and three different developers making the same mistake at the same time stretches the coincidence story past the breaking point. Greystoke has already said they will submit corrected figures, which is what you do when the original numbers were never going to survive scrutiny.

Google made a public sustainability commitment in 2020 to operate on 24/7 carbon-free energy by 2030, and the company has been quietly walking it back as AI compute demand has exploded. The Essex submissions are what that walk-back looks like in practice. When the commitments were profitable to make, they were front and center in every press release. Now that the math no longer works, the same companies are filing planning documents that obscure the actual emissions and hoping the councils approving them are not staffed to catch it. The planning process only works when the numbers being submitted are honest, and a factor-of-five understatement on three different projects is not the kind of thing that happens without someone choosing to let it happen.

text:
🦔Google is rolling out an AI-powered health coach on May 19, built on Gemini and bundled into the rebranded Google Health Premium subscription at $9.99/month or $99/year. The Fitbit app is being absorbed into "Google Health," with existing users migrating automatically. The AI coach analyzes activity, sleep, nutrition, and wearable data to generate personalized fitness plans, log meals from photos, and integrate medical records in select regions. The launch coincides with the Fitbit Air, a $99 screenless wearable, and the bundle is included free with Google AI Pro and Ultra subscriptions in over 30 countries. Google says health data will not be used for advertising.

My Take
Consumer health chatbots have an 80%+ error rate on differential diagnosis according to peer-reviewed studies, and the Joe Riley story from a few months back showed where that goes wrong, with his father dying after refusing cancer treatment based on a hallucinated Perplexity report. Gemini is not magically immune to this, and packaging it as a "health coach" rather than a diagnostic tool is the legal hedge that lets Google ship it while shifting liability to the user when it gives bad advice. The wellness framing is doing real work here.

The "health data will not be used for advertising" promise is technically true and practically misleading. Google does not need to use your health data for ads when the data is what makes the rest of the Google ecosystem more valuable, since insurers, employers, and Google's own AI training pipelines all benefit from a richer behavioral profile of you. Fitbit data has been part of the Google acquisition stack since 2021, and the same regulators who pushed back on that deal are nowhere to be found on this rollout. My honest read is that this product will be genuinely useful for people who want structured exercise plans and sleep insights, and dangerous for people who use it to replace seeing a doctor when something is actually wrong. Knowing which category you fall into is on you, because Google's marketing is built to blur that line on purpose.

text:
🦔A data center in Fayetteville, Georgia, drained approximately 30 million gallons of water through two industrial-scale hookups that the local utility did not know existed. One connection had been installed without the utility's knowledge, and the other was not linked to any account and therefore was not being billed. The discovery only came after residents complained about low water pressure. 
The campus is still under construction with completion projected three to five years out. A separate incident in Tucson last week saw Project Blue's contractor caught trucking municipal water out of a city that had explicitly voted against the project, with Tucson revoking the temporary meter and demanding two acre-feet of water credits to make the city whole.

My Take
Two unrelated data center water incidents in two weeks across two different states is a pattern, not a coincidence. The Georgia facility was running off an unmetered industrial hookup nobody at the utility had on file, which means either a contractor installed it without authorization or the utility lost track of a connection serving a major customer, and neither of those explanations should make anyone comfortable. The construction phase alone consumed 30 million gallons before operations even began, which gives you a sense of the water demand profile these facilities have once they go live.

The bigger issue is that hyperscale data centers are being permitted under regulatory frameworks built for industrial users a fraction of their size, and the utilities responsible for tracking water use are not staffed for facilities this scale. A 30 million gallon discrepancy slipping through billing is not a clerical error, it is a sign that the infrastructure for monitoring these projects is being outpaced by the speed at which they are being built. Tucson caught their problem because a citizen made a phone call to a council staffer, and Fayetteville caught theirs because neighbors noticed their taps had lost pressure. Neither of those is a functioning compliance system, and the next community in this situation will probably not catch it at all.

text:
🦔A photo circulating online shows a printed computer science textbook with ChatGPT's signature phrasing left intact in the body text, including "If you want, I can also explain columns, primary keys, or other DBMS terms" sitting between sections explaining database concepts. The textbook appears to be from South Asia. 
The image is the latest in a series of documented incidents this year, including the Chicago Sun-Times printing an AI-generated summer reading list with books that did not exist, Hachette Book Group canceling the novel Shy Girl in early 2026 over suspected AI use, a UCLA professor defending her AI-generated textbook after students flagged it as error-ridden, and Elsevier publishing a peer-reviewed paper that contained obvious ChatGPT artifacts in the abstract.

My Take
A textbook is supposed to be the part of education that has been vetted, edited, and signed off on by people who know the subject, which is the entire reason students pay for them. Finding ChatGPT's conversational scaffolding printed in a database textbook means the publisher cut the editorial chain, ran the manuscript through an LLM, and shipped it without anyone reading the output. Pair that with the Sun-Times running a syndicated insert that nobody at the paper reviewed because they assumed Hearst had editors, and the UCLA professor charging students $25 for an AI textbook she calls a success, and you can see what is actually happening across publishing, journalism, and academia all at once.

The editorial function is being eliminated as a cost line, and the savings are being captured by publishers while the quality cost is being passed to readers who do not yet know enough to spot the errors. A student trusting a textbook has no signal to tell them which sentences are accurate and which are hallucinated, and the same is true of a newspaper reader trusting a reading list, or an academic citing a peer-reviewed paper. The traditional checks on what got printed were not perfect, but they existed, and they are being removed faster than the institutions relying on them can adapt.

text:
(I recommend reading this for anyone currently invested in the market or interested in learning what's going on)

🦔$2.6 trillion in S&P 500 call options traded in a single session yesterday, the highest single-day notional volume in the history of the index. The chart above shows what that growth curve actually looks like, with daily call volume sitting under $200 billion as recently as 2019 and now running at multiples of that on a regular basis. The combination of zero-day-to-expiration options, retail platforms that gamified options trading, and algorithmic market makers running delta-neutral books has turned the largest equity index in the world into something closer to a leveraged casino than a price discovery mechanism.

My Take
I want to walk through what is actually happening here because most retail traders chasing these calls do not understand the machinery on the other side of their trades. When you buy a call option, a market maker like Citadel or Jane Street sells it to you and then buys the underlying stock to hedge their exposure. As the stock price rises, they buy more shares to stay neutral, which pushes the price higher, which pulls in more retail call buyers, which forces more hedging. This is called a gamma squeeze, and it works in both directions. The same mechanic that drives prices up violently can pull them down even faster when the flow reverses, which is why these moves tend to end in sharp drawdowns rather than gentle corrections.

The piece that worries me as someone who covers this stuff is how much of this volume is concentrated in zero-day options that expire the same session. A retail trader buying a $5 call that expires at 4 PM is taking a position with leverage that would have required a margin account and a series-7 license twenty years ago, and the platforms now make it as easy as ordering takeout. Market makers love this volume because they make money on the spread regardless of direction, but the buyers on the other side are absorbing all the risk in a structure designed to extract pennies from them at scale. If you take nothing else from this post, understand that $2.6 trillion in daily call volume is not a sign the market is healthy, it is a sign that speculation has overwhelmed investment, and historically that pattern resolves in ways that hurt the people who came late to it. I am not telling anyone what to do with their money, but knowing how the machinery works is how you avoid being the exit liquidity for someone else's algorithm.

text:
🦔The City of Tucson has revoked a temporary water meter Project Blue's contractor was using to truck Tucson drinking water out to a data center construction site that the City Council had explicitly rejected last August. Beale Infrastructure's contractor Ames Construction obtained the meter on April 24, then transported the water outside Tucson's service area to a project Pima County had separately approved over city objections. 
City Manager Tim Thomure asked Beale Infrastructure to make the city whole with two acre-feet of water credits. Project Blue says it is procuring construction water from an alternative source going forward. Arizona has been in drought conditions for over thirty years, and the project has faced sustained protests over environmental impact and transparency.

My Take
Building a hyperscale data center in the Sonoran Desert was already an aggressive choice given the water constraints, but trucking municipal drinking water out of a city that voted unanimously against the project is the kind of behavior that turns local opposition into national news. Beale's contractor obtained a permit through normal channels and used it in a way the city had specifically rejected, which technically followed procedure while completely violating the spirit of the council vote. The fact that a citizen tip triggered the investigation rather than any internal compliance check tells me the developer was banking on nobody noticing.

I honestly keep coming back to how predictable this story is. Pima County approved the project over the city's objections, the developer told everyone Project Blue could not be built without Tucson water, and then the moment construction started they found a workaround that involved using Tucson water anyway. That sequence is a useful preview of how the AI infrastructure buildout is going to play out in every drought-stressed jurisdiction with weak coordination between county and municipal authorities. Hyperscale data centers consume water at a scale that does not fit the existing regulatory categories, and developers are exploiting the gaps faster than local governments can close them. Tucson got lucky that someone was paying attention, but most communities in Project Blue's situation will not.

The company is also dropping its founding policy of women initiating conversations and rolling out an AI dating assistant called "Bee" that learns user preferences and acts as a personal matchmaker. 
The changes come as Bumble's paid user base dropped 21% in Q1 2026 to 3.2 million from 4 million a year earlier, though revenue per paying user rose 7.9% to $22.20. CEO Whitney Wolfe Herd called the user decline a "deliberate reset" prioritizing quality over quantity.

I do not trust a company whose entire business model depends on subscription retention to optimize an algorithm for getting you off their platform, because the financial incentive runs the other way every time.

Calling a 21% drop in paid users a "deliberate reset" is the kind of spin that does not survive contact with the actual numbers. Bumble grew on the premise that women initiated conversations, and abandoning that founding feature alongside the swipe at the same moment the user base is collapsing reads to me like a company that ran out of ideas and is hoping the AI label buys it a few quarters of investor patience. Dating app fatigue is genuine across the entire category, and replacing the swipe with a black box does not fix the underlying problem that people are exhausted by being products inside an engagement loop.

text:
🦔The April jobs report came in at 115,000 payrolls added, beating the Bloomberg consensus of 65,000 and keeping unemployment at 4.3%. March was revised up from 178,000 to 185,000, but February was revised down to a loss of 156,000 positions. Healthcare and social assistance added 54,000 jobs, couriers and messengers added 38,000, and information sector employment fell 13,000 with the category now down 342,000 since November 2022. 
People working part-time for economic reasons jumped 445,000 to 4.94 million, and the unemployment rate at the second decimal moved from 4.26% in March to 4.34% in April.

My Take
The headline beat looks strong until you see where the jobs came from. Healthcare, courier work, and social assistance accounted for the majority of additions, which are sectors that grow regardless of cycle because they track demographics and consumer logistics rather than business investment. Information, finance, and manufacturing all shed jobs, which are the categories that actually signal economic expansion. A report where the growth is concentrated in delivery drivers and home health aides while white collar employment shrinks is not the same kind of report as one where hiring is broad-based, even if the topline number reads the same.

The 445,000 jump in people working part-time because they cannot find full-time work is involuntary part-time employment, and a half-million-person move in a single month is a pace usually associated with the early innings of a downturn. Combined with steady downward revisions to prior months and an information sector that has lost 11% of its workforce in three years, the picture underneath the 115,000 print is an economy where the jobs being created are lower-quality, more precarious, and concentrated in fewer sectors than the headline suggests. The report is not bad, but it is not what the markets cheered, and the revisions next month will likely bring consensus closer to what the underlying data already shows.

text:
🦔SoftBank has cut its planned margin loan backed by OpenAI shares from $10 billion to roughly $6 billion, a 40% reduction, after creditors raised concerns about valuing an unlisted company as collateral. The two-year loan was originally pitched in April with an option to extend an additional year. 

SoftBank invested in OpenAI in September 2024 and partnered with the company on the Stargate AI infrastructure initiative announced in January 2025. The conglomerate also secured a separate $40 billion bridge loan in March to fund further OpenAI investments and general corporate purposes.

My Take
The circular financing on display here is the cleanest example I have seen all year of how AI capital is actually flowing. SoftBank borrowed $40 billion in March to invest in OpenAI, is now borrowing another $6 billion against the OpenAI shares it acquired with that first loan, and will likely deploy those proceeds into more OpenAI investment through Stargate, which in turn supports the valuation of the very shares being used as collateral. The same dollar gets counted as investment, as collateral, and as future commitment, and every link in the chain depends on the OpenAI valuation continuing to rise.

This is the same pattern showing up across the sector. Nvidia invests in OpenAI, OpenAI buys Nvidia chips, OpenAI's revenue gets cited to justify Nvidia's growth story. Microsoft, Anthropic, Google, and Oracle all have versions of this running in parallel. A 40% haircut on a margin loan because lenders cannot agree on collateral value shows me the people closest to the underwriting are starting to price the risk differently than the headline valuations suggest, which is the first crack you usually see in arrangements like this. SoftBank survived WeWork because the rest of the portfolio absorbed the loss. The OpenAI exposure is structured at a scale where I am not sure that backstop holds.

🦔Residents living near AI data centers are reporting constant low-frequency hum measured as infrasound, sound below the human hearing threshold that causes dizziness, nausea, vertigo, and sleep disruption. The noise comes from cooling systems and onsite gas turbines hyperscalers are building behind-the-meter to bypass slow grid interconnects. 
Local zoning rules do not regulate infrasound because traditional noise ordinances only measure decibels in the audible range, leaving residents with no legal recourse. The video below captures what households near these facilities are living with around the clock.

My Take
The infrasound problem does not exist in the regulations because the technology producing it at scale did not exist when the rules were written, and developers know exactly which rural jurisdictions lack the framework to push back. 
Households who bought before the data center was announced get to choose between selling at a loss or living with symptoms that did not exist a year ago, and that cost is being externalized onto people who never agreed to host critical infrastructure for the AI buildout.

text:
🦔Canadian youth unemployment hit 19.5% for 15-19 year olds in 2025, with 437,000 young Canadians out of work, a 57% increase in three years and a level historically only seen during severe recessions. Real GDP grew 1.7% over the same period, so this is happening outside a downturn. 
The gap with US youth unemployment is the widest on record outside the late-1990s tech boom and the pandemic. A Fraser Institute report attributes the surge to a 14.3% increase in the youth population from 2021 to 2025 driven by non-permanent residents and foreign students, and minimum wages up 124.5% nationally between 2005 and 2024.

My Take
This is what happens when immigration policy and wage policy run on opposite assumptions about labor supply. Bringing in non-permanent residents and foreign students at four times the level of permanent immigration during a period when entry-level vacancies were already collapsing was always going to land hardest on the workers with the least seniority. Retail and food service hired 7.4% more adults while youth employment in those same sectors fell 4.6%, which shows you who employers chose when they had the option.

The lesson worth absorbing on the US side is that the entry-level pipeline is more fragile than policymakers tend to model. Canada built theirs around assumptions about postpandemic labor demand that did not hold, and rebuilding it for the 19-year-olds who just lost their first three years of work experience is going to take a decade.

text:
🦔Span is partnering with Nvidia and homebuilder PulteGroup to install "mini data centers" on the walls of new homes, using Span smart panels, batteries, and Nvidia compute nodes to turn unused household electrical capacity into distributed AI infrastructure. 
AI companies would rent inference compute from thousands of residential nodes, and homeowners would receive discounted energy, internet, and backup power in exchange. A 100-home pilot is planned for 2026. Span argues the model could ease AI's power bottleneck by tapping existing residential grid capacity rather than waiting years for centralized data centers to come online.

My Take
The reason this idea exists is that the centralized data center model is hitting hard physical limits, with construction debt overwhelming bank balance sheets, transmission queues stretching past 2030, and rural communities increasingly fighting hyperscale projects in court. Distributing inference compute to residential nodes solves the permitting problem by routing around it entirely, since a panel on the side of a house does not require a township vote or a 1.4 gigawatt grid interconnect. The homeowner becomes the permit, which is the product feature Nvidia is actually selling here.

The economics for the homeowner do not work as cleanly as the pitch suggests. Inference workloads need 24/7 utilization to justify the hardware cost, and most residential solar surplus runs maybe four hours a day on the best days, which means Nvidia is going to be drawing from grid power the homeowner pays for and offering discounted electricity that may or may not cover the differential. The hardware sitting on the wall is worth thousands of dollars in resale value, which creates theft incentives the model cannot really design around without making the homes harder to live in. My read is that this is a real solution to Nvidia's permitting and capex problem and a questionable deal for the homeowner, and the 100-home pilot will tell us whether the contracts are written tightly enough to externalize the risk to PulteGroup buyers who probably will not read them.

text:
🦔A $16 billion OpenAI and Oracle data center is under construction in Saline Township, Michigan, after the local planning commission and township board both voted to reject the project in September. Two days after the rejection, developer Related Digital sued the township for exclusionary zoning, and within weeks the township settled rather than face legal costs that could have triggered tax assessments on residents. 
The 21 million square foot project will draw 1.4 gigawatts of electricity and broke ground in November before OpenAI and Oracle publicly announced it as part of Stargate. Governor Whitmer met virtually with Sam Altman in spring 2025, months before the proposal reached the township. The settlement gave Saline $14 million in community benefits against a $16 billion project budget.

My Take
Local zoning should determine what gets built in a community, and Saline shows how that breaks down when the developer outspends the municipality's entire legal budget. The board voted 4-1 against the project, residents showed up to oppose it, and Related Digital's lawsuit hit the docket within 48 hours of the rejection. That timeline only works if the legal team had everything drafted before the public vote, which means the developer treated the local process as theater from the start.

The bigger issue is that AI infrastructure gets sold as a national priority while the costs land entirely on the local level. Saline residents absorb the truck traffic, the diesel generators that run during outages, the strain on private wells, and 1.4 gigawatts of new electricity demand DTE will have to source from somewhere. OpenAI, Oracle, and Related Companies capture the upside, and the construction jobs disappear once the buildout finishes. $14 million in community benefits against a $16 billion project budget is less than one tenth of one percent, which gives you a sense of how the parties valued the township's leverage. The financing side is the only realistic constraint left on this build cycle, which is why bank offloading of data center debt and the new private credit CDS contracts deserve more attention than they are currently getting.

text:
🦔Five major publishing houses (Elsevier, Cengage, Hachette, Macmillan, McGraw Hill) and author Scott Turow sued Meta and Mark Zuckerberg in federal court Tuesday, alleging Zuckerberg personally authorized the illegal use of millions of copyrighted books and journal articles to train Llama.
Authors represented include James Patterson, Donna Tartt, Joe Biden, and two of this year's Pulitzer Prize winners. Meta's defense rests on the position that AI training on copyrighted material qualifies as fair use, citing recent court rulings. The case follows Anthropic's $1.5 billion settlement last year in a similar suit, with a final approval hearing scheduled for next week.

My Take
The fair use defense Meta is leaning on does not actually settle this question, because the recent Anthropic ruling specifically held that training on copyrighted works can be fair use only when those works were obtained legally. Meta is alleged to have used pirated databases including LibGen, which is the part of the complaint that distinguishes this case from the cleaner test cases the industry would prefer to litigate. The $1.5 billion Anthropic paid to settle a smaller scale of infringement is a useful benchmark for what Meta's exposure could look like if the publishers prove the works were obtained from pirate sources.

The two-tier justice frustration in the public response is fair, because an individual torrenting one movie can face statutory damages of $150,000 per work, while a trillion-dollar company allegedly downloading millions of pirated books to build a commercial product is treated as a civil dispute that gets settled in dollars the company barely notices on its balance sheet. The structural issue is that copyright enforcement was designed for a world where the infringer was usually smaller than the rightsholder, and the AI training era has inverted that completely. My read is that Meta will fight this hard because settling sets a precedent for every other dataset they used, and the publishers know it, which is why this case is going to be defended on principle rather than dollars on both sides.

That gap will keep widening as long as rates stay here.

The math does not work for a household earning median income looking at a median-priced home, and the longer rates stay here the more first-time buyers age out of the formation cycle entirely. 
There are downstream effects on household formation, birth rates, and the entry-level construction market that nobody in the housing complex wants to talk about, because the answer requires either rates falling significantly or prices falling significantly, and the industry is structured to prevent both. Transaction volumes stay low, existing owners stay locked in, and a generation of would-be buyers continues paying rent that funds someone else's mortgage.

text:
🦔LinkedIn research covering 8,500 working professionals found that 72% now question whether a job posting is legitimate before applying, and 29% always do. The FTC reports losses tied to job scams have climbed into the hundreds of millions, and Gen Z is nearly twice as likely as Gen X to fall for one, with nearly a third admitting they ignore warning signs because opportunities feel scarce. More than a third of recruiters report being impersonated. The overwhelming majority of reported scam messages involve attempts to move conversations off LinkedIn into private messaging apps, usually in the first exchange.

My Take
The job scam problem on LinkedIn is a downstream symptom of how thin the entry-level labor market has become, because scarcity is what makes people skip verification steps they would normally do automatically. Gen Z is not less savvy than Gen X, they are more desperate, and a generation looking at 42.5% graduate underemployment and 92,000 tech layoffs in Q1 alone is going to engage with offers they would otherwise dismiss. Scammers know this and have moved their entire playbook to where the desperation lives.

Recruiter impersonation at this scale points to AI-generated profiles, AI-written outreach, and deepfaked video interviews becoming standard tooling for fraud, and the same technology being sold to recruiters to streamline hiring is being used against the people they are trying to hire. LinkedIn catching 98.7% of scam content sounds impressive until you remember the volume has risen fast enough that the remaining 1.3% is still flooding inboxes. The verification push is necessary but it does not address the underlying problem, which is that desperate applicants and AI-armed scammers are both products of an economy that is not changing course anytime soon.

text:
🦔HP is now requiring a monthly subscription to use the print function on certain consumer printers, with users reporting that machines they already paid for are demanding ongoing payments to continue printing. This follows years of HP using firmware to block third-party ink, designating cartridges as empty when they still contain ink, and burning color ink during black and white print jobs. Brother laser printers remain one of the few consumer options that still work as standalone hardware without ongoing fees.

My Take
Charging a subscription for the print function on hardware a customer already paid for is the logical endpoint of two decades of slow capture, where the printer stopped being a product you owned and became a billing relationship with a device on your desk. HP's lawyers will say the EULA covered this, and they will be technically right, but nobody buying a $200 printer at Best Buy reads thirty pages of terms to discover that the machine they are about to install can be remotely converted into a metered service whenever the company decides quarterly earnings need a lift.

This model is spreading well beyond printers. GM put Gemini in 4 million vehicles with telematics consent buried in the agreement, Honda is now charging a subscription for garage door functionality that used to ship in the remote, and the FTC just signaled it has limited interest in pursuing data-sharing concerns in connected cars. Hardware companies have figured out that the highest-margin business is the one where the customer keeps paying after the sale, and the regulators who should be drawing a line on what counts as a reasonable post-purchase change in functionality are not drawing it. My honest read is that consumers will eventually push back the way they did on cable bundles and ad-supported streaming, but the time between when companies start doing this and when the market punishes it is long enough that a generation of devices gets sold under terms most buyers never agreed to in any meaningful sense.

text:
🦔Microsoft's Windows 11 Recall feature is back after the 2024 backlash that forced its removal, and security researcher Alexander Hagenah has demonstrated that his TotalRecall Reloaded tool can run in the background, force user authentication prompts, and extract everything Recall has captured. Microsoft denies any security flaw exists. The return arrives alongside an always-listening Copilot microphone integration, with Recall continuing to capture continuous screenshots of user activity including passwords, banking sessions, and private messages.

My Take
Microsoft pulling Recall in 2024 after security researchers tore it apart and quietly bringing it back eighteen months later with the same fundamental architecture is a textbook case of a company waiting out the news cycle rather than fixing the problem. The vault is real, the encryption is real, and the trust boundary still ends in a place that lets a researcher with a public GitHub tool walk out with the contents. When the company response is to deny the flaw exists rather than engage with the proof of concept, you are looking at a product that ships because executives need an AI feature to point at, not because the engineering team thinks it is ready.

The combination of Recall capturing screenshots and Copilot sitting on the microphone is where my view of Windows 11 changes. An operating system used to be the layer that ran your software, and now it is increasingly the layer that watches what you do so the vendor can train models, sell ads, or both. France moved 2.5 million government PCs to Linux this year, and the IT departments I respect are disabling Recall through group policy by default rather than trusting the security model. My honest take is that the surveillance capabilities being baked into consumer Windows are now a meaningful enterprise risk, and the calculus for staying on the platform is shifting for anyone whose threat model includes their own operating system as a potential adversary.

text:
🦔Senators Adam Schiff and Mike Rounds introduced the LIFT AI Act, a bipartisan bill directing the National Science Foundation to fund AI literacy curricula and teacher training across K-12 schools. The bill is endorsed by Google, OpenAI, Microsoft, HP, and the American Federation of Teachers. 
The NSF has been without a director for a year, all 22 members of the National Science Board were fired last week without explanation, and Trump's nominee to run the agency is a former Peter Thiel employee with no research background. Research from all 50 states shows student reading and math scores reversed from rising to falling after digital technology was widely adopted in classrooms, with the timing tracking the rollout rather than COVID.

My Take
A bill that puts the largest AI companies in the world in charge of writing the curriculum that teaches children how to use their products is not education policy, and calling it bipartisan because both parties signed on does not change what it is. Google, OpenAI, and Microsoft built business models that depend on a generation of users who default to AI for thinking, writing, and research, and handing them influence over K-12 standards is the regulatory equivalent of letting Philip Morris design health class in 1965.

The data on classroom devices is unambiguous and Congress has already been shown it. Every state that rolled out digital tech saw test scores flip from rising to falling, and putting AI into those same classrooms is going to make outcomes worse, not better. The AFT signing on for $23 million from Microsoft, OpenAI, and Anthropic to build an AI training hub for teachers tells you which side of the table the union has chosen, and the kids being experimented on do not get a vote. My honest read is that the entry-level workforce being dismantled by AI today and the K-12 cohort being trained to rely on it tomorrow are the same problem at different points in the timeline.

text:
🦔Google Chrome has been silently downloading a 4GB Gemini Nano AI model to user devices without consent, and Chrome automatically redownloads it if deleted. Computer scientist Alexander Hanff has formally accused Google of violating European privacy regulations and estimates the rollout transferred several exabytes globally, generating up to 60,000 metric tons of CO2. The kicker is that Chrome's most visible AI feature runs on Google's servers anyway, with the local 4GB model only used for writing assistance and features buried several menus deep.

My Take
Pushing 4GB of unrequested data to billions of devices to enable a feature most users will never touch reflects a deliberate choice about whose resources matter and whose do not. Google consumed user storage, bandwidth, and electricity at planetary scale rather than ask whether anyone wanted Gemini Nano installed, because asking would have produced the wrong answer. Up to 60,000 metric tons of CO2 for a writing assistant nobody requested is the kind of number that should embarrass a company still running sustainability ads.

The bigger issue is that the largest software companies now treat user devices as extensions of their own infrastructure. Microsoft did it with Windows Recall, Anthropic was caught installing a native messaging bridge through Claude Desktop without consent, and Google is using Chrome's auto-update channel to push model weights nobody asked for. The cost gets externalized to your storage, your power bill, and your data cap, and my read is that this only stops when regulators treat unrequested local installs the same way they treat unrequested data collection.

text:
🦔California farmers are destroying 420,000 peach trees following Del Monte's bankruptcy earlier this year. The successor company that bought Del Monte is purchasing 50,000 fewer tons of peaches from the region, and canned peach demand has been declining for over a decade as consumers shifted to fresh and frozen alternatives. 
The fruit is still edible, but the trees were planted specifically for canning and the cultivars do not store or transport well enough to compete on grocery shelves against varieties bred for that market. Total revenue loss to the region is estimated at $550 million, and growers are expected to replant with pistachios and almonds.

My Take
There is a real Grapes of Wrath instinct that kicks in when you read a headline about hundreds of thousands of fruit trees being torn out, and I understand it. A canning peach picked for the Del Monte line is not going to survive the journey to a Kroger produce section looking like something a shopper would pay for, which is why farmers grow different cultivars for fresh sale. The real loss is a regional economy built around a category of shelf-stable food Americans stopped buying, along with the workers, processors, and trucking routes that depended on it.

The replanting decision is where this gets harder to feel good about for me. California is shifting toward pistachios and almonds, both of which use considerably more water than peach trees, in a state that has spent the last decade in cycles of severe drought and groundwater depletion. SGMA regulations are tightening, Central Valley aquifers are dropping fast enough to cause land subsidence and well failures in farming communities, and growers are still being pushed toward higher-margin tree nuts because that is where the money is right now. My concern is that the bankruptcy made the news but the replanting choice will produce a much bigger problem in five to ten years, and almost nobody is covering that part.

text:
🦔Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong announced a 14% workforce reduction today, framing it as a response to both market cyclicality and what he called the AI inflection point. The plan flattens the org to five layers max below CEO/COO with leaders carrying 15+ direct reports, eliminates pure managers in favor of player-coach ICs, and concentrates around AI-native pods including experiments with one-person teams handling engineering, design, and product.
Armstrong wrote that non-technical teams are now shipping production code at Coinbase. Severance is 16 weeks base pay plus two weeks per year of service, the next equity vest, and six months of COBRA. Coinbase stock is down roughly 50% from its July 2025 peak, and the company recently received a banking charter.

My Take
Two things can be true at once. Armstrong is likely seeing real AI productivity gains in his engineering org, the severance package is among the more generous in this layoff cycle, and a flatter structure with fewer pure managers is defensible for a company that wants to move faster. The issue is the language used to dress it up, because "rebuilding Coinbase as an intelligence with humans around the edge aligning it" is the kind of sentence written when you are trying to make a cost cut sound visionary, and the people he just laid off can read it as well as everyone else.

The line that deserves more attention is non-technical teams shipping production code, because Coinbase is a regulated financial institution that just received a banking charter and holds customer assets that can disappear permanently if the code is wrong. There is a meaningful difference between AI helping engineers ship faster and product managers committing to main without a senior reviewer, and conflating the two in a public memo invites the kind of regulatory scrutiny a newly chartered bank does not need. 
My read is that the layoff is defensible on cost grounds with COIN down 50% from last summer and crypto volumes soft, but trying to say it is AI transformation rather than a margin response is the same playbook a dozen CEOs have run this year, and the market has stopped giving credit for it.

text:
🦔Printed circuit board prices jumped as much as 40% in April according to Goldman Sachs, with copper foil up 30% year to date. The trigger was an Iranian strike on Saudi Arabia's Jubail petrochemical complex that halted production of a critical resin used in PCBs. 
Lead times for some chemicals have stretched from three weeks to fifteen, and AI infrastructure demand is competing with consumer electronics for the same constrained supply. IDC and Wedbush expect the pass-through to consumers in summer and fall.

My Take
One missile strike in the Gulf is flowing straight to the bill of materials on a laptop sold in Ohio, which shows how fragile the post-pandemic supply chain rebuild actually was. Jubail produces a specific epoxy resin used in nearly every circuit board manufactured globally, with no substitute and no inventory buffer, because the diversification everyone talked about after 2021 happened on assembly and never reached chemical inputs.

AI server demand was already absorbing PCB capacity before the conflict, with hyperscalers willing to pay almost any price to keep buildouts on schedule. Consumer electronics makers do not have that pricing power, so they will eat margin first and raise prices second, and mid-range phones, laptops, and appliances are where it lands. IDC is calling this a structural multi-year upcycle, and the cost floor for electronics is moving up regardless of when the conflict resolves.

text:
🦔A 2024 Harris Poll found 43% of millennials have chosen or will choose pets over children, with finances cited as the main reason by 42% of respondents. A new Gen Z survey put the share prioritizing pets over starting a family at 62%. 
Raising a child to 18 in the US now costs around $414,000 versus roughly $5,000 a year for a pet. 67% of US households have at least one pet while only 39% have a child under 18, and the Trump administration is reportedly weighing a $5,000 baby bonus to encourage births.

My Take
Calling this a values shift misses what is happening on Gen Z balance sheets, because a generation looking at $414,000 to raise one child while paying $1,800 a month for a one-bedroom and servicing $35,000 in student loans is not making a lifestyle statement when they choose a dog instead, they are doing arithmetic. 
A $5,000 baby bonus looks like policy designed by people who have not priced a year of childcare lately, where one infant in daycare in most metros runs $20,000 to $30,000 before housing, healthcare, or the income hit one parent typically absorbs in the early years.

The bigger problem is that this is a slow-moving demographic crisis for a lot of developed economies, with South Korea at a fertility rate of 0.72, Japan and Italy at 1.20, and the US at 1.62 against a replacement rate of 2.1. A country with a shrinking working-age population and a growing retired one ends up with fewer workers funding more pensions, weaker consumer demand, and a tax base that cannot support the safety net previous generations were promised. 
The countries that have actually moved birth rates did it through housing affordability, paid parental leave measured in months, and subsidized childcare, none of which is seriously on the table here. My view is that the Gen Z preference for pets is a rational response to an economy that has made the alternative unaffordable, and the bill for ignoring it arrives thirty years from now in a labor force that cannot support the generation currently writing the policy.

text:
🦔Banks including JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, MUFG, and SMBC are shopping ways to offload data center debt exposure, with lenders spending more than six months trying to distribute $38 billion of construction debt tied to a single Oracle-leased project in Texas and Wisconsin. Some banks have sold portions to non-bank lenders at a discount. 
The structures being explored include modified significant risk transfers, where banks slice individual concentrated loans rather than pooling dozens of smaller ones, with deals in the $500 million range backed by a single borrower already in market. Maine passed a statewide data center moratorium in April, adding regulatory risk to projects that already carry construction risk and heavy concentration among a handful of operators.

My Take
Banks selling loans at a discount to clear them off the books shows how tight things have gotten internally, because JPMorgan does not spend six months shopping $38 billion in construction debt unless risk limits are getting hit and the only way to keep originating new loans into the AI buildout is to make room first. Man Group describing banks as starting to choke on the size of these deals is not language that ends up in print by accident.

A traditional SRT spreads risk across dozens of loans so no single default sinks the trade, while what is being shopped now is closer to a single-borrower SRT with investors taking the riskiest tranche of one concentrated loan to one operator carrying significant construction risk and one or two anchor tenants. That looks closer to the bespoke single-name structures that showed up before 2008 than to the diversified portfolio transfers Europeans have used for years, and my question is what happens when the AI capex cycle slows and you are left with half-built data centers in secondary markets tied to operators whose business models depend on frontier model spending continuing forever. The banks are doing what rational risk managers should do, and the fact that they need to is what should be getting attention.

text:
🦔Foreclosures jumped 26% year over year in Q1 2026 to 119,000 filings, the highest since early 2020. The driver is not bad lending or collapsing prices but exploding ownership costs, with property taxes, insurance premiums, and HOA fees stacking on top of mortgages taken out at peak prices and rates that never came down. 
Buyers from 2021 to 2025 are most exposed, and HUD pulled pandemic-era FHA loss mitigation rules on October 1, 2025. Loan modifications at current rates often raise payments rather than lower them, leaving stretched borrowers with fewer exits than in any recent downturn.

My Take
119,000 filings in a quarter is nowhere near the 900,000 annual peak of 2008, and anyone using this print to call an imminent housing collapse is overstating it. Foreclosure data also lags by months, so this reflects stress that built up through last year rather than what is happening on balance sheets right now.

The composition of that stress is what changes my view of where it goes from here. In 2008 the loans themselves were the problem and you could see the fraud in the paperwork before prices broke. This time the loans are mostly fine and the houses are mostly worth what people paid, but everything around the mortgage got more expensive at once. Insurance in Florida and parts of California is repricing climate risk in real time, property taxes are catching up to pandemic valuations on a lag, and HOAs that used to be a nuisance line item are filing liens over a few thousand dollars in arrears. 
None of that shows up in a credit model, and none of it gets fixed by the Fed cutting 50 basis points. People bought a payment they could afford and are now paying for things they could not have priced in at signing, and the safety nets that caught households through the pandemic have been quietly dismantled.

text:
🦔California's DMV has announced new regulations allowing police to issue notices of noncompliance directly to autonomous vehicle manufacturers when their cars violate traffic laws, effective July 1. Companies will be required to respond to police and emergency officials within 30 seconds and face penalties if their vehicles enter active emergency zones. 
Previously, officers who stopped a driverless car committing a violation had no mechanism to issue a ticket without a driver present. In one documented case, San Bruno police watched a Waymo make an illegal U-turn directly in front of them and could only contact the company about the glitch. San Francisco Fire Department officials have repeatedly complained about robotaxis blocking emergency responses.

My Take
Driverless cars have been operating on public roads without any mechanism for traffic enforcement, which is a regulatory failure that should never have existed. The autonomous vehicle industry moved faster than the legal framework, something I've watched repeat across AI deployment all year. The 30-second response requirement and manufacturer liability structure are reasonable starting points, but they don't address the more fundamental question of what happens when an AI system causes harm that goes beyond a traffic ticket.

A Waymo blocking an intersection during a medical emergency, or stalling in a blackout and gridlocking a city, is a different category of consequence than a speeding fine. The manufacturer notification system works for moving violations. It becomes much harder to apply when the question is who bears liability for an outcome that an algorithm produced with no human in the loop to hold accountable.

text:
🦔CEO pay for S&P 500 executives increased 25.6% between 2024 and 2025 while inflation-adjusted wages for average private sector workers grew 1.3%. CEOs now earn 281 times more than the typical worker, up from a ratio of 60 three decades ago, with average total compensation at $22.98 million. 
Cumulative inflation over four years sits at 16%. 59% of Americans live paycheck to paycheck, 56% say everyday life has become less affordable, and the purchasing power of the federal minimum wage has decreased 21% since 2019.

My Take
CEOs took home 25.6% more last year while their companies cut workforces and told workers AI efficiency justified the restructuring. Oracle hired a CFO at $950,000 base and $26 million in stock the same week it cut 30,000 employees. Meta posted $26.8 billion in net income last quarter and is cutting 8,000 people. I don't think the 281 to one ratio happened by accident. It happened through compensation committee decisions, stock buyback programs that inflate executive equity awards, and a federal minimum wage that has lost a fifth of its purchasing power since 2019 while remaining unchanged.

I've watched companies all year use AI as cover for workforce reductions that funnel savings directly into shareholder returns and executive compensation. The surveillance wages research showed employers already use financial vulnerability data to suppress what workers get offered. 59% of Americans living paycheck to paycheck while CEOs pocket 281 times the median worker salary reflects decades of policy choices that consistently favor capital over labor, and AI is accelerating that concentration rather than reversing it.

text:
🦔A Boston startup called Alpha School has opened an AI-powered school promising students will complete academics in two hours a day with no teachers, replaced by "guides" who monitor progress. Each student gets a laptop with an AI tutor that adapts to their level. The school has the backing of Melania Trump and Education Secretary Linda McMahon. 
A February investigation by 404 Media found the school's AI system generated questions about sections not in the article, questions with multiple plausible correct answers, and questions that contradicted other parts of the same text. The school also maintains a spreadsheet of student names, grades, and webcam recordings that was accessible to anyone on the internet with the link.

My Take
We covered research showing fifteen years of laptops in Maine classrooms produced no improvement in test scores, and schools across the country reversing tech-first policies after reading comprehension declined. Alpha School is doubling down on the same approach that failed, adding AI tutors to replace the teachers whose absence was already a problem, and doing it at the kindergarten level.

The two hours a day claim is what I find most concerning as an education proposition. Elementary school is not primarily about academic content delivery. It is where children learn to sit with frustration, navigate conflict with peers, follow instructions from an adult who isn't their parent, and develop the social infrastructure that every subsequent stage of life depends on. An AI tutor optimized for academic throughput cannot provide any of that. The founders of this school are solving the wrong problem with the wrong tool and the children enrolled in it are the ones who will find out years from now what was missing.

text:
🦔New Mexico's AG filed for injunctive relief against Meta ahead of a May 4 bench trial, seeking sweeping changes to how Meta operates for users under 18. Meta threatened to pull Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp from the state entirely rather than comply. A Santa Fe jury already found Meta liable for 75,000 violations of New Mexico's Unfair Practices Act and ordered $375 million in penalties in March, the first state trial win against a major tech company for endangering children. 

The case originated from a 2023 undercover operation where a fake 13-year-old profile was immediately flooded with adult solicitations with no algorithm flagging it. Meta's own internal documents showed employees calculated that Zuckerberg's 2019 decision to roll out end-to-end encryption by default would affect detection of approximately 7.5 million child sexual abuse material cases annually.

My Take
A company that threatens to leave an entire state rather than implement child safety measures has made its priorities clear. The AG noted that Meta has rewritten its own rules, redesigned its products, and bent to the demands of authoritarian governments to preserve market access in other contexts. The claim that New Mexico's requirements are technically impossible is hard to take seriously from a company with that track record.

The internal document where Meta employees calculated that end-to-end encryption would affect detection of 7.5 million child sexual abuse cases annually, and Zuckerberg rolled it out anyway, is something I believe should follow every Meta child safety statement indefinitely. 
More than 40 state attorneys general have sued Meta over child safety and federal legislation has stalled repeatedly. New Mexico won at trial and then pursued injunctive relief because federal inaction left states to act alone, and Meta's answer was to threaten to take its platforms and leave rather than protect the children on them.

text:
🦔Anthropic quietly doubled its estimate of what Claude Code costs enterprise developers, from $6 per active day to $13, with costs below $30 per day for 90% of users. The previous estimate was based on Sonnet 3.7 as the primary model. 
Anthropic says the updated figures reflect Opus 4.7 now being the primary Claude Code model and that there was no pricing change. At $13 per active day, monthly costs run $150-250 per developer. Anthropic's head of growth recently acknowledged that current subscription plans weren't built for current usage levels, with engagement per subscriber described as way up.

My Take
Calling this a model upgrade rather than a price increase is technically accurate and practically irrelevant to any finance team looking at the line item. If the cost to do the same work doubled because the model got better, the budget impact is identical. The companies that cut engineers to fund AI subscriptions based on one cost number are now operating against a cost structure that is a different number, and every data point this year points in the same direction.

This is the circular financing problem made concrete. Anthropic loses money on every token sold, raises prices as usage scales, and the startups and enterprises building on top absorb the increase with no corresponding guarantee that the productivity case they sold their boards has materialized. 
Uber's CTO blew through his annual AI budget before the year was half over. Goldman found inference costs approaching headcount parity. GPU spot prices up 48%. The cost of the AI bet keeps rising while the evidence that it's paying off remains, by the industry's own admission, largely unproven. I've been watching this compress all year and the direction has not changed once.

text:
🦔US public debt surpassed the size of the economy for the first time since World War II, reaching 100% of GDP as of March 31 with debt at $31.27 trillion against GDP of $31.22 trillion. The post-WWII record of 106% was set in 1946 during demobilization. 

The Congressional Budget Office projects debt will break that record by 2030 at 108% and reach 120% by 2035. The CBO also projects debt will grow faster than GDP in the years ahead, which it says could slow economic growth, reduce private investment, and increase interest costs. The annual deficit is projected to reach $3 trillion within the decade.

My Take
In 1946 the US had just won the largest military conflict in human history and was demobilizing a wartime economy. The debt was the cost of survival and victory. The Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget noted that this time the borrowing comes from a bipartisan abdication of making hard choices rather than a seismic global conflict. That distinction matters because the political will to reduce a wartime debt is fundamentally different from the political will to reduce a debt accumulated through decades of spending commitments nobody wanted to cut and tax cuts nobody wanted to reverse.

With debt now above 100% of GDP and the annual deficit heading toward $3 trillion, the pressure on whoever runs the Fed to accommodate Treasury borrowing rather than fight inflation is going to be significant. The Iran war is already pushing energy prices higher and tariff inflation is already in the pipeline. A Fed chair who prioritizes coordination with a Treasury financing $39 trillion in debt at elevated interest rates, in an inflationary environment, is a combination that historically has not ended well for price stability or for ordinary people whose wages don't keep up with what that combination produces.

text:
🦔A Cast AI analysis of roughly 23,000 Kubernetes clusters found average GPU utilization across enterprises sits at 5%, meaning 95% of provisioned GPU capacity is idle. CPU utilization averages 8% and memory 20%. Companies are overprovisioning out of fear of missing allocations rather than sustained demand. The CEO of Cast AI described it plainly: companies are overbuying GPUs out of fear of missing out.

My Take
Last night Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, and Alphabet all reported earnings beating expectations while simultaneously raising AI infrastructure spend. Meta alone raised its 2026 capex guidance to $125-145 billion. GPU spot prices are up 48% in two months. CoreWeave raised rates 20%. The narrative driving all of that is insatiable AI demand that companies cannot afford to miss.

Against that backdrop, 95% of provisioned enterprise GPU capacity sitting idle is a figure I find genuinely difficult to square with the shortage narrative. The price increases and multi-year infrastructure commitments are being driven by demand signals from companies that purchased GPUs on the same fear-of-missing-out logic and are now leaving 95% of that capacity unused. 
Companies are paying for GPUs they aren't running while bidding up the price of GPUs they claim they urgently need. The hyperscalers booking record backlogs last night are counting commitments from the same enterprises sitting on idle infrastructure, which is a different thing from collected revenue, and the distinction is important when the bill for all of it eventually comes due.

text:
🦔The Federal Reserve held rates steady at 3.5% to 3.75% today in what may have been Jerome Powell's final meeting as chair, but the vote split 8-4, the most dissents since October 1992. One governor dissented in favor of a cut. Three regional presidents dissented in the opposite direction, objecting to language in the statement implying the next move would be lower. 

The committee noted inflation remains elevated partly due to rising global energy prices. Markets are pricing in no cuts for the rest of 2026 and well into 2027. The Senate Banking Committee advanced Kevin Warsh's nomination as the next Fed chair on a party-line vote earlier today.

My Take
An 8-4 split is not a routine disagreement. Four dissents in the same meeting pulling in opposite directions describes a committee that has genuinely lost consensus on where policy should go. That level of internal division makes forward guidance harder to interpret and increases uncertainty for anyone making borrowing or investment decisions based on where rates are headed.

The broader challenge is that the Fed is caught between two real problems simultaneously. The Iran war has pushed energy prices high enough and long enough that officials can no longer treat it as a temporary shock to look through, which argues for holding or tightening. At the same time the labor market is softening and consumer sentiment is at record lows, which argues for easing. Warsh has advocated for a closer relationship between the Fed and Treasury on debt issuance, which could complicate that already difficult balance if the incoming chair prioritizes coordination over price stability at a moment when inflation remains well above the 2% target.

text:
🦔GM is rolling out Google Gemini to approximately four million vehicles via over-the-air update, replacing Google Assistant across 2022 and newer Cadillac, Chevrolet, Buick, and GMC models. The deployment is one of the largest in-car AI rollouts in the automotive industry. 
This comes one year after the FTC took action against GM and OnStar for collecting and selling precise geolocation and driving behavior data to insurance companies without clear consumer consent, resulting in a five-year consent order barring GM from selling such data without explicit permission.

My Take
GM was sanctioned by the FTC a year ago for selling driving behavior data to insurers without drivers knowing, causing insurance premiums to rise for people who had no idea their data was being harvested. The response is to deploy an AI assistant that by design accesses vehicle data, learns personal preferences, and integrates with OnStar's connectivity infrastructure across four million cars via an update most owners won't fully understand they're receiving.

I covered the Stellantis and Microsoft partnership last week and the broader direction is consistent across the industry. Cars are becoming data collection platforms that also happen to transport you. The difference with GM is that we already know exactly what they did with that data the last time they had it, and a consent order that bars them from selling it for five years is not the same as a company that has decided not to. The privacy controls GM is promising will be judged by whether defaults favor the driver or the data pipeline, and given the history, I wouldn't assume the answer without reading the fine print carefully.

text:
🦔 OpenAI missed its internal goal of one billion weekly active users for ChatGPT by end of 2025 and missed several monthly revenue targets earlier this year after losing market share to Anthropic. OpenAI's CFO Sarah Friar disclosed to company leaders concerns the firm may be unable to pay for future computing contracts if revenue growth fails to accelerate. Friar also warned OpenAI may be unable to meet public company reporting standards required for an IPO, while CEO Sam Altman prefers an aggressive timeline. 

Shares of OpenAI's investors and partners fell on the news: CoreWeave down 7.1%, Oracle down 6.5%, SoftBank down 11.9%, Broadcom down 4%, Nvidia down 3%, Microsoft down 1%. OpenAI's current valuation sits at $852 billion following a $122 billion funding round in March.

My Take
OpenAI raises $122 billion at an $852 billion valuation while losing money on every token sold, commits $600 billion to computing infrastructure through 2030, and signs contracts with Oracle, CoreWeave, and Nvidia whose valuations rise on the back of those commitments, while those same companies are investors in OpenAI. That circular structure has been the foundation of the AI boom's financial architecture and now the CFO is privately telling leaders the company may not be able to fund the contracts it already signed.

I think the IPO is the moment this entire structure gets stress tested against people who weren't already inside it. Friar's private caution about whether OpenAI can meet basic public reporting standards, while Altman pushes for an aggressive timeline, is the kind of internal tension that tends to surface badly once there are public shareholders asking questions. An $852 billion valuation was never built on demonstrated revenue and I think anyone whose pension fund or retirement account is exposed to the companies built on top of OpenAI's projected growth should be paying close attention to how that IPO actually comes together.

text:
🦔About 70% of college students see AI as a threat to their job prospects according to Harvard Kennedy School polling, and many are switching majors in search of AI-proof careers. A business analytics student switched to marketing to build critical thinking and interpersonal skills. A data science major is considering switching to studio art. A computer science graduate applied to 50 software engineering jobs without a single interview before pivoting to a master's degree. 
The president of Brown University told a Stanford panel that universities need to think hard about what students need to succeed in 10 to 20 years, then added that none of them know the answer.

My Take
The Brown University president admitting she doesn't know what students should learn is the most honest thing a university administrator has said publicly about this moment. These institutions are charging students $60,000 to $80,000 a year for degrees while having no idea whether those degrees will lead anywhere. The student switching from data science to studio art because she'd rather be unemployed doing something she loves than unemployed doing something she doesn't is a rational response to an irrational situation, and I find it hard to argue with her logic.

The students trying to navigate this don't have bad instincts. Critical thinking, relationship building, and communication genuinely are harder to automate than statistical analysis and coding. The problem is that the same companies cutting data scientists and coders are also cutting marketing teams, communications roles, and middle management. There is no major that is AI-proof because the disruption isn't sector-specific. It's structural. And the universities collecting tuition while admitting they don't have answers to the question their students are paying them to answer are a meaningful part of that problem.

text:
🦔Nvidia's VP of applied deep learning told Axios that for his team, the cost of compute is far beyond the cost of employees. An MIT study found AI automation is economically viable in only 23% of roles where vision is a primary part of the work, meaning human labor remains cheaper in the vast majority of cases. Uber's CTO said he's back to the drawing board because his 2026 AI budget is already blown. AI software fees have increased 20% to 37% over the past year. Despite all of this, Big Tech has announced $740 billion in capital expenditures for AI this year, a 69% increase from 2025, while laying off more than 92,000 tech workers.

My Take
The core contradiction of the AI moment is now being stated openly by people inside the industry. Companies are cutting human workers who are cheaper than the AI replacing them, to fund AI infrastructure that isn't generating measurable productivity returns, financed by investors who are also funding the AI companies selling the tokens at prices those companies cannot sustain without continued subsidy.

My honest read is that the workforce decisions being made right now are irreversible on a timeline the technology cannot meet. The entry-level pipeline being dismantled today will take a decade to rebuild. The engineers being cut to fund token budgets that exceed their salaries are the same people who would catch the failures when the AI gets it wrong. Companies are making permanent structural changes based on a cost structure that doesn't exist yet and a productivity case that by their own executives' admission hasn't materialized. At some point the distance between the bet and the reality has to close, and the people who absorbed the cost of being wrong won't be the ones who made the decision.

text:
🦔The founder of PocketOS, a SaaS platform for car rental businesses, watched an AI coding agent delete his entire production database and all backups in 9 seconds. The agent was Cursor running Claude Opus 4.6, assigned to a routine task in a staging environment. When it hit a credential issue, it decided on its own to delete a Railway infrastructure volume to fix the problem, not realizing the volume was shared across environments. 
Railway's architecture made it worse as the API executes destructive commands without confirmation, backups are stored on the same volume as the source data, and wiping a volume deletes all backups simultaneously. The founder has spent days helping customers manually reconstruct months of booking data from Stripe receipts and email confirmations. A three-month-old backup exists but everything since is gone.

My Take
The agent's own post-incident confession is the most clarifying thing in this story. It said it guessed that deleting a staging volume would be scoped to staging only, didn't verify, didn't check if the volume ID was shared across environments, and ran a destructive command without being asked. It knew it violated every principle it was given.

I've watched companies all year give AI systems increasing autonomy while simultaneously cutting the engineers who would have caught these failures before they cascaded. The AWS incident in December, the Amazon AI sprawl story, and now this follow the same trajectory. The tools are being deployed faster than anyone is thinking through what appropriate guardrails look like, at customers' expense, in production environments where the consequences are irreversible. Giving an AI agent administrative credentials and disabling confirmation prompts because you want it to work without being watched is a reasonable description of how most of these deployments are actually being run right now. The industry is discovering the hard way that confidence without verification is as dangerous in an AI system as it is in a junior engineer.

text:
🦔The FAA has brought in Palantir, Thales, and Air Space Intelligence to compete on an AI tool for air traffic management as part of a $12.5 billion congressional appropriation to modernize the nation's aging air traffic control system, with an estimated $20 billion more needed to complete the overhaul. The tool would use AI to predict air traffic flows, adjust departure times to resolve conflicts, and alert controllers when aircraft are approaching too close to each other.

My Take
The case for AI in air traffic management is genuinely more defensible than most of the deployments I cover. Conflict detection and flow prediction are bounded, high-volume pattern recognition tasks with structured data and human controllers remaining in the loop. That's the application profile where AI has demonstrated capability rather than confidence without competence.

What I'd watch is the procurement process and the transition period. Palantir's government contract history includes significant gaps between what was promised and what was delivered at scale. The FAA's existing systems are notoriously fragmented, which makes integration failure modes difficult to predict in testing and potentially catastrophic in production. A system that works in a controlled environment and fails during a Northeast corridor ground stop on a holiday weekend is a different order of consequence than most software failures. The $12.5 billion already allocated with $20 billion more requested also deserves scrutiny given the history of large government technology procurements, which have a consistent record of delivering late, over budget, and under spec.

text:
🦔Business Insider is calling the current tech job market an AI air pocket, suggesting some of the pressure may be temporary as companies pause hiring while evaluating AI tools, with cuts potentially slowing after the AI infrastructure buildout peaks around 2028. Challenger estimates AI directly accounts for about a quarter of this year's cuts, with the rest attributed to over-hiring corrections and budget reallocation toward AI investment. The permanent changes being cited are consolidation of middle management, decline of process-focused roles, and new categories like AI agent orchestration emerging to replace them.

My Take
I'd push back a bit about calling it an air pocket. The 90% of CEOs reporting no productivity impact from AI after three years, the Solow paradox comparisons, the enterprise adoption data showing most workers aren't using the tools at all, none of that points to a temporary evaluation pause that resolves cleanly in 2028. What's happening looks more like companies using a genuine period of AI experimentation as cover for structural workforce reductions they wanted to make anyway, while the productivity case for those reductions remains unproven.

The permanent changes being described, fewer middle managers, more builders, agent orchestration roles, are real as a directional shift but the timeline and scale are being significantly overstated. The entry-level pipeline being dismantled right now is what produces the experienced workers and managers of 2030 and 2035. Companies betting they can skip that pipeline and fill senior roles from an AI-augmented talent pool that never had to develop judgment through lower-level work are making an assumption that has no historical precedent to support it.
