text:
🦔O'Leary Digital is nearing final approval for a hyperscale data center project on 40,000 acres in rural Box Elder County, Utah. The first phase requires 3 gigawatts of power, nearly matching Utah's entire average statewide consumption of 4 gigawatts. At full buildout it reaches 9 gigawatts, more than double the state's current total energy use. All power will come from natural gas via the Ruby Pipeline. To attract hyperscalers, Utah's Military Installation Development Authority slashed the energy use tax from 6% to 0.5%, directed 80% of property tax revenue back to the developer, and eliminated personal property taxes on equipment. Box Elder County commissioners, who learned of the proposal only weeks ago, postponed their final vote after expressing concern about the speed of the process and requesting resident input.

My Take
Festus Missouri removed half its city council after approving a $6 billion data center without adequate public review. Maine passed an 18-month moratorium. Virginia's public opinion has turned sharply against new development. The communities that have fared worst are the ones that moved fastest under pressure from developers who treated speed as a virtue and scrutiny as an obstacle.

A project consuming more power than an entire state, built entirely on natural gas in the watershed of a lake that has already lost half its volume over 30 years, with 80% of property tax revenue redirected back to the developer, deserves more than a few weeks of review from commissioners who described feeling like they were drinking from a fire hose. The jobs and tax revenue arguments sound compelling in a presentation but the fine print on who actually captures the value in these deals consistently differs from the headline numbers.

text:
🦔Toronto-Dominion Bank is exploring a Significant Risk Transfer deal to hedge approximately $1 billion in data center debt, with a forward-flow arrangement that would allow the size to grow over time as it originates more data center loans. 
TD joins Societe Generale and Morgan Stanley in using SRTs to offload data center financing risk. An SRT acts as insurance against default, typically covering 5% to 15% of a loan portfolio's value, allowing banks to free up capital for new lending while transferring the default risk to outside investors.

My Take
An SRT is a bank saying it wants the fee income from originating data center loans but doesn't want to hold the default risk on its own balance sheet. The outside investors buying that risk are typically hedge funds and institutional investors, many of whom are the same pension funds and endowments already stretched by private credit redemption requests and PE zombie portfolios we've covered.

TD moving to hedge $1 billion in data center exposure through a forward-flow structure, meaning the hedging grows as they keep lending, is a bank building an exit ramp before it needs one. Societe Generale and Morgan Stanley are doing the same. When multiple major banks simultaneously start transferring risk out of the same asset class, it lets me know something about how those banks privately assess that asset class. The satellite imagery showed 40% of data center projects missing completion dates. The electrical component shortages are documented. The community opposition is hardening. The banks financing the buildout are now packaging that risk and selling it to someone else. That doesn't like a vote of confidence in the underlying infrastructure thesis to me.

text:
🦔Meta has signed a deal to use millions of Amazon Graviton CPUs for AI workloads, shifting spend back to AWS after a $10 billion Google Cloud deal last August. The Graviton is a CPU rather than a GPU, reflecting how agentic AI workloads like real-time reasoning and multi-step task coordination require different chips than model training. 
Amazon's Trainium GPUs were already committed to Anthropic in a $100 billion ten-year deal earlier this month, making Graviton the available alternative for Meta. The deal puts Amazon in direct competition with Nvidia's Vera CPU, which targets the same agentic inference workloads.

My Take
Step back and look at what's actually happening here. Anthropic committed $100 billion to Amazon over ten years while losing money on every token sold. Meta is spending hundreds of billions on AI infrastructure while cutting the human workforce that generates its revenue. Amazon is using both deals to justify its chip investment and compete against Nvidia, which it simultaneously buys from. The valuations underpinning all of it, Anthropic at $61 billion, Meta at over a trillion, AWS priced as an AI infrastructure winner, depend on a productivity wave that 90% of CEOs say hasn't materialized yet.

The CPU versus GPU shift for inference is a genuine technical development and if agentic AI drives the next compute wave, controlling inference-optimized chips will matter enormously. But the capital commitments being made to get there are extraordinary, locked in for decades, between companies that are simultaneously each other's customers, competitors, and creditors. The circular financing doesn't resolve because one chip wins. It resolves when someone has to show the returns, and nobody in this deal has a clean answer to that question yet.

text:
🦔Schools across the US are reversing years of technology-first classroom policies after studies show laptop and screen use has either decreased test scores or produced no improvement. Maine adopted one-to-one laptop policies in 2002 and showed no improvement after 15 years. 
Neuroscientist Jared Cooney Horvath told the Senate that frequent in-class computer use correlates with significantly lower math and science scores across both high and middle income countries, and that Gen Z is the first generation in modern history to score lower than their parents on standardized tests. Schools in Kansas, North Carolina, Michigan and elsewhere are restricting laptop use and returning to pen and paper, with some reporting improvements in reading comprehension within months.

My Take
The data here is hard to argue with. Fifteen years of laptops in Maine classrooms produced no improvement in test scores. Schools that switched back to pen and paper saw reading comprehension improve within months. The technology industry spent billions convincing schools that screens were the future of learning, and the evidence is pointing in the opposite direction.

We're pulling laptops out of elementary classrooms in 2026 at the same moment we're deploying AI into hospitals, courtrooms, and financial systems that depend on humans being able to think critically, catch errors, and exercise judgment. The children who spent their formative years navigating text boxes instead of working through problems on paper are the same people who will be asked to oversee those AI systems in ten years. If the screen-first approach genuinely stunted the development of analytical thinking it was supposed to enhance, we have a compounding problem that goes well beyond test scores.

text: It looks like glass, yet it won’t shatter when dropped🥃 In Yao, Osaka, Kinjo Rubber uses silicone molding to create a refined, easy-to-use cup. Drop-resistant and accessible to all, it joins the YAOYA Project to showcase Yao and help energize local manufacturing✨ https://t.co/fSqJ9a67rz

text: Azaleas🌺 are now in full bloom across Japan. In shades of red, pink, purple, and white, they brighten shrines, temples, and parks, with seasonal festivals held in some locations. These vibrant scenes look almost like paintings. Why not experience their fleeing beauty yourself? https://t.co/7DUyh092dI
label: high

text: Could a simple cream be a breakthrough against malaria? Japan’s Kao Corporation has developed an innovative mosquito repellent with a brand-new approach to global health—starting right at home🏡. Learn more about its potential impact! https://t.co/ebZQr7VIPa
